summary: Validate a machine learning model with k-fold cross-validation
description: Evaluates a machine learning model using k-fold cross-validation. The training data is split into k folds; the model is trained k times, each time using k-1 folds for training and the remaining fold for validation. Aggregated performance metrics are written to ``metrics.json`` in the job directory. The model is then refit on the full training set and returned with cross-validation scores attached in its metadata, so it can be used directly for prediction with ``ml_predict()`` or persisted with ``save_ml_model()``.

parameters.name: model
parameters.description: A machine learning model to evaluate. Must be an untrained model specification (for example from ``mlm_class_random_forest()`` or ``mlm_class_tempcnn()``) that supports cross-validation and training.

parameters.name: training_data
parameters.description: Training samples used for cross-validation and for the final refit. Can be provided as a URL to a data file, a local file path, or an inline data object depending on the backend implementation.

parameters.name: folds
parameters.description: Number of folds for cross-validation. Common values are 5 or 10.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: The name of the variable in the training data that serves as the ground truth for evaluation. Set to `null` for unsupervised or self-supervised tasks.

parameters.name: scoring
parameters.description: One or more metrics to include in ``metrics.json``. Supported overall metrics include 'accuracy', 'kappa', and related confidence statistics. Per-class metrics such as 'f1', 'precision', 'recall', 'sensitivity', and 'specificity' are included when requested.

parameters.name: seed
parameters.description: Random seed for reproducibility of fold splits and model training.

returns.description: The model refit on the full training set, enriched with cross-validation scores in its metadata. The model can be directly used for prediction with ``ml_predict()``.

links.title: Arlot & Celisse (2010) - A Survey of Cross-Validation Procedures for Model Selection
